color_profiles.name: Classic Alexandrite Color Change
colors: emerald-green to bluish-green in daylight; red to purplish-red in incandescent light
color_profiles.cause: Cr3+ in BeAl2O4 orthorhombic field: dual transmission windows in red and blue-green of visible spectrum; daylight (blue-rich) emphasizes green transmission; incandescent (red-rich) emphasizes red transmission.

color_profiles.name: Brazilian Alexandrite
colors: brownish-green to brownish-red; less vivid than Russian
color_profiles.cause: Same Cr3+ mechanism; slight Fe contamination shifts color toward brown.

color_profiles.name: Cat's Eye Alexandrite
color_profiles.cause: Color change from Cr3+ combined with chatoyancy from parallel tube inclusions or needle inclusions.

visual_notes: Alexandrite is unique — a single stone with two identities. Under northern daylight or fluorescent light it is distinctly green; under incandescent (candle, tungsten) light it turns vivid red-purple. The best Russian specimens shift from 'emerald' to 'ruby' — an impossible color change nicknamed 'the emerald by day, ruby by night' gem. No other natural gemstone produces such a dramatic and complete color change.

optical_effects.fluorescence: Moderate red fluorescence under UV (Cr3+ emission, same mechanism as ruby)
optical_effects.chatoyancy: Cat's eye alexandrite — strong chatoyancy from oriented needles; combines color change + cat's eye
optical_effects.asterism: Extremely rare four-rayed star alexandrite documented
